caps.proposition_text: The clinician assesses iron deficiency anemia.

evidence.span_text: Iron deficiency anemia.

caps.proposition_text: The patient reports feeling unable to catch their breath.

evidence.span_text: The patient reports she has been living with anemia since she was 13 years old. She attempted to donate blood on 12/2019 but was unable to due to her anemia. She has a history of heavy menstruation and a hysterectomy in 1996. She completed a colonoscopy 5-6 years ago with nonsignificant findings. She denies having a pacemaker, defibrillator, sleep apnea, or using oxygen.

caps.proposition_text: The clinician recommends an appointment with a hematologist.

evidence.span_text: Schedule upper endoscopy, colonoscopy and appointment with hematologist. Return to the clinic if symptoms worsen.

caps.proposition_text: The clinician recommends scheduling an upper endoscopy.

evidence.span_text: Schedule upper endoscopy, colonoscopy and appointment with hematologist. Return to the clinic if symptoms worsen.

caps.proposition_text: The patient reports experiencing tingling sensations.

evidence.span_text: Mrs. Cooper reports a myriad of symptoms, stating she feels fatigued often during the day, feverish with chills at times, and during activity feels she cannot catch her breath and some wheezing is present. She has been having some headaches which is uncommon for her. Additionally, she is noticing tingling sensations, cold sensitivity, and her anxiety and depression have worsened.

caps.proposition_text: The patient reports experiencing cold sensitivity.

caps.proposition_text: The clinician recommends vitamin C in orange juice.

evidence.span_text: I have asked her to continue each ferrous sulfate dose with vitamin C found in orange juice for the next 12 weeks then recheck CBC, iron ferritin, B-12, and folate. A referral was sent for her to see a hematologist. We will plan for EGD and colonoscopy to assess for potential sources of anemia or GI bleed. If this is inconclusive, capsule endoscopy can be considered.

caps.proposition_text: The clinician recommends continuing ferrous sulfate dose.

caps.proposition_text: The clinician observes normal iron labs.

evidence.span_text: Mrs. Cooper is a 65-year-old female who was diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia in 12/2019 and was unable to donate blood. Her follow-up blood work in 01/2020 revealed a low hemoglobin, stable hematocrit, and normal iron labs, although, ferritin was low. She has been taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg by mouth.

Mrs. Cooper is a 65-year-old female who was diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia in 12/2019 and was unable to donate blood. Her follow-up blood work in 01/2020 revealed a low hemoglobin, stable hematocrit, and normal iron labs, although, ferritin was low. She has been taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg by mouth.

Mrs. Cooper is a 65-year-old female who was diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia in 12/2019 and was unable to donate blood. Mrs. Cooper is a 65-year-old female who was diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia in 12/2019 and was unable to donate blood. Her follow-up blood work in 01/2020 revealed a low hemoglobin, stable hematocrit, and normal iron labs, although, ferritin was low. She has been taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg by mouth.

I have asked her to continue each ferrous sulfate dose with vitamin C found in orange juice for the next 12 weeks then recheck CBC, iron ferritin, B-12, and folate. A referral was sent for her to see a hematologist. We will plan for EGD and colonoscopy to assess for potential sources of anemia or GI bleed. If this is inconclusive, capsule endoscopy can be considered.

caps.proposition_text: The patient takes Ferrous Sulfate 25 mg tablet twice per day.

evidence.span_text: Ferrous Sulfate 25 mg tablet twice per day (1 in the morning, 1 in the evening), Vitamin B12 OTC.

caps.proposition_text: The patient takes Vitamin B12 OTC.
